content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 33 (NUM_033)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 33:1 -- "journeys" vs. "stages of the journey"; "by their hosts" vs. "according to their hosts" vs. "by their divisions"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "These [are] the journeys of the sons of Israel who went out from the land of Egypt by their hosts, by the hand of Moses and Aaron." |
| LOGOS | "These are the journeys of the sons of Israel who went out from the land of Egypt according to their hosts, by the hand of Moses and Aaron." |
| PNEUMA | "These are the stages of the journey of the sons of Israel who came out of the land of Egypt by their divisions, under the hand of Moses and Aaron." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "These are the journeys of the sons of Israel who went out from the land of Egypt by their hosts, by the hand of Moses and Aaron." -- The Hebrew mas'ey (plural) is best rendered "journeys" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's periphrastic "stages of the journey." PNEUMA's "divisions" for tsiv'otam diverges from "hosts" (EMET) and "hosts" (LOGOS); the Hebrew tsava denotes a military host/army, and "hosts" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA's "under the hand" interprets beyad as subordination, while "by the hand" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew idiom more faithfully. PNEUMA's "came out" vs. "went out" (EMET/LOGOS) for yatse'u is a minor divergence; "went out" is adopted as the majority reading. LOGOS's "according to" for the preposition le is slightly interpretive; EMET's "by" is more neutral and adopted.

---

### 33:2 -- "wrote" vs. "wrote down" vs. "recorded"; "goings out" vs. "starting points"; "by the mouth of YHWH" vs. "by the command of YHWH"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Moses wrote their goings out according to their journeys by the mouth of YHWH; and these [are] their journeys according to their goings out." |
| LOGOS | "And Moses wrote down their starting points according to their journeys by the command of YHWH; and these are their journeys according to their starting points." |
| PNEUMA | "And Moses recorded their starting points, stage by stage, at the command of YHWH. And these are their stages according to their starting points:" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses wrote down their starting points according to their journeys by the command of YHWH; and these are their journeys according to their starting points." -- (1) "wrote down" (LOGOS) is adopted over "wrote" (EMET) and "recorded" (PNEUMA) as a close but natural rendering of vayyikhtov. (2) "starting points" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better captures the Hebrew motsa'eihem ("their going-forth points") than EMET's archaic "goings out." (3) "by the command of YHWH" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's hyper-literal "by the mouth of YHWH" for al-pi YHWH; while "mouth" is the literal sense, "command" is the established idiomatic meaning. (4) PNEUMA restructures the chiastic second clause into "their stages according to their starting points" and adds a colon, departing from the Hebrew parallelism. The LOGOS rendering preserves the chiasm: "starting points...journeys / journeys...starting points."

---

### 33:3 -- "journeyed" vs. "set out"; "on the morrow of the Passover" vs. "on the day after the Passover"; "with a high hand" vs. "with a raised hand"; "in the eyes of" vs. "in full view of"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they journeyed from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month; on the morrow of the Passover the sons of Israel went out with a high hand in the eyes of all Egypt." |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month -- on the day after the Passover the sons of Israel went out with a high hand in the eyes of all Egypt," |
| PNEUMA | "And they set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month -- on the day after Passover the sons of Israel marched out with a raised hand, in full view of all Egypt." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month -- on the day after the Passover the sons of Israel went out with a high hand in the eyes of all Egypt," -- (1) "set out" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "journeyed" (EMET) for vayyis'u, as it better captures the sense of departing from a named location. This verb choice applies throughout the itinerary sections. (2) "on the day after the Passover" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's archaic "on the morrow of the Passover"; PNEUMA drops the article ("Passover" without "the"), but the Hebrew has ha-Pesach with the article. (3) "with a high hand" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "with a raised hand" for beyad ramah; "high hand" is the established English idiom for this Hebrew phrase. (4) "in the eyes of all Egypt" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew le'einei better than PNEUMA's "in full view of," which paraphrases. (5) PNEUMA's "marched out" for yatse'u adds military connotation not present in the Hebrew verb; "went out" is adopted.

---

### 33:4 -- "And Egypt was burying" vs. "while the Egyptians were burying"; "struck" vs. "struck down"; "upon their gods YHWH had executed judgments" vs. "for YHWH had executed judgments against their gods"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Egypt was burying [those] whom YHWH had struck among them, every firstborn; and upon their gods YHWH had executed judgments." |
| LOGOS | "while the Egyptians were burying those whom YHWH had struck down among them, every firstborn; and upon their gods YHWH executed judgments." |
| PNEUMA | "And the Egyptians were burying those whom YHWH had struck down among them -- every firstborn -- for YHWH had executed judgments against their gods." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "while the Egyptians were burying those whom YHWH had struck down among them, every firstborn; and upon their gods YHWH had executed judgments." -- (1) "while the Egyptians" (LOGOS) captures the circumstantial clause construction; EMET's "And Egypt" is hyper-literal (the Hebrew uses mitsrayim which can mean "Egypt" or "the Egyptians"). PNEUMA uses "And the Egyptians." LOGOS's "while" best conveys the Hebrew participial construction as simultaneous action. (2) "struck down" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "struck" (EMET) for hikkah, as the context (death of firstborn) warrants the intensified form. (3) The word order "upon their gods YHWH had executed judgments" (EMET) preserves the Hebrew fronting of "upon their gods" (uveloheihem); LOGOS similarly preserves this. PNEUMA restructures to "for YHWH had executed judgments against their gods," changing the conjunction from "and" to "for" (interpreting waw as causal) and moving "their gods" to the end -- a significant departure from the Hebrew word order. The EMET/LOGOS word order is adopted with "had executed" to preserve the pluperfect sense.

---

### 33:5 -- "journeyed" vs. "set out"; "encamped" vs. "camped"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the sons of Israel journeyed from Rameses and encamped at Succoth." |
| LOGOS | "And the sons of Israel set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth." |
| PNEUMA | "And the sons of Israel set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the sons of Israel set out from Rameses and encamped at Succoth." -- "Set out" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "journeyed" (EMET) for vayyis'u as the standard rendering throughout the itinerary. For vayyachanu, EMET's "encamped" is preferred over "camped" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) as more precise for the Hebrew root chanah, which specifically denotes setting up camp (pitching tents), not merely stopping. "Encamped" is adopted as the standard verb for the entire itinerary formula. This resolution applies to all formulaic itinerary verses (5-37, 41-49) and will not be repeated for each occurrence.

---

### 33:7 -- "turned back to" vs. "turned back toward"; "which [is] before Baal-zephon" vs. "which faces Baal-zephon"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they journeyed from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, which [is] before Baal-zephon, and they encamped before Migdol." |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, which is before Baal-zephon, and they camped before Migdol." |
| PNEUMA | "And they set out from Etham and turned back toward Pi-hahiroth, which faces Baal-zephon, and they camped before Migdol." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from Etham and turned back to Pi-hahiroth, which is before Baal-zephon, and they encamped before Migdol." -- (1) "to" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "toward" for the directional he on the place name; the arrival is completed, not merely intended. (2) "which is before" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "which faces" for asher al-penei; "before" is the standard spatial rendering. PNEUMA's "faces" personifies the location.

---

### 33:8 -- "crossed over through" vs. "crossed through"; "midst" vs. "middle"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they journeyed from before Hahiroth and crossed over through the midst of the sea into the wilderness" |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from before Hahiroth and crossed through the midst of the sea into the wilderness" |
| PNEUMA | "And they set out from before Hahiroth and crossed through the middle of the sea into the wilderness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from before Hahiroth and crossed through the midst of the sea into the wilderness" -- (1) EMET adds "over" ("crossed over through"), which is redundant with "through"; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "crossed through" is cleaner. (2) "midst" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "middle" (PNEUMA) for betokh, as "midst" better preserves the formal register and the Hebrew sense of being surrounded by the sea on both sides.

---

### 33:8 -- "went a journey of three days" vs. "traveled three days' journey"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and they went a journey of three days in the wilderness of Etham" |
| LOGOS | "and they went a journey of three days in the wilderness of Etham" |
| PNEUMA | "and they traveled three days' journey into the wilderness of Etham" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and they went a journey of three days in the wilderness of Etham" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. PNEUMA's "traveled" for vayyelechu and "into" for the preposition be are minor divergences; "went" and "in" are more literal. The possessive "three days' journey" (PNEUMA) is a stylistic variant of "a journey of three days" (EMET/LOGOS); the construct-chain rendering is adopted.

---

### 33:9 -- "and in Elim [were]" vs. "And at Elim there were"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they journeyed from Marah and came to Elim; and in Elim [were] twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees" |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from Marah and came to Elim; and in Elim were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees" |
| PNEUMA | "And they set out from Marah and came to Elim. And at Elim there were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from Marah and came to Elim; and in Elim were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they encamped there." -- EMET and LOGOS use a semicolon to join the clauses; PNEUMA breaks into a new sentence and uses "at Elim there were" with an existential construction. The Hebrew uv'Eilim ("and in Elim") is better served by the EMET/LOGOS rendering with "in Elim" rather than PNEUMA's "at Elim there were."

---

### 33:14 -- "and there was no water there" vs. "and there was no water there" (semicolon vs. comma)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And they journeyed from Alush and encamped at Rephidim; and there was no water there for the people to drink." |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim; and there was no water there for the people to drink." |
| PNEUMA | "And they set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim, and there was no water there for the people to drink." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from Alush and encamped at Rephidim; and there was no water there for the people to drink." -- All three agree on the content. PNEUMA uses a comma where EMET and LOGOS use a semicolon. The semicolon better marks the shift from itinerary formula to editorial note.

---

### 33:36 -- "wilderness of Zin -- that [is] Kadesh" vs. "that is, Kadesh"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in the wilderness of Zin -- that [is] Kadesh" |
| LOGOS | "in the wilderness of Zin, that is, Kadesh" |
| PNEUMA | "in the wilderness of Zin -- that is, Kadesh" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in the wilderness of Zin -- that is, Kadesh." -- The em-dash (EMET/PNEUMA) better signals the explanatory gloss than LOGOS's comma construction. EMET's bracketed "[is]" is unnecessary since "that is" is a standard English parenthetical. The PNEUMA punctuation is adopted.

---

### 33:37 -- "on the edge of the land of Edom" vs. "on the border of the land of Edom"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "on the edge of the land of Edom" |
| LOGOS | "on the edge of the land of Edom" |
| PNEUMA | "on the border of the land of Edom" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "on the edge of the land of Edom" -- The Hebrew qetseh means "end, extremity, edge." EMET and LOGOS agree on "edge," which is more literal than PNEUMA's "border" (which implies a political boundary). "Edge" is adopted.

---

### 33:38 -- "went up to Mount Hor" vs. "went up Mount Hor"; "by the mouth of YHWH" vs. "at the command of YHWH"; "of the going out" vs. "after...had gone out" vs. "after...had come out"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Aaron the priest went up to Mount Hor by the mouth of YHWH and died there, in the fortieth year of the going out of the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first [day] of the month." |
| LOGOS | "And Aaron the priest went up to Mount Hor at the command of YHWH and died there, in the fortieth year after the sons of Israel had gone out from the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first day of the month." |
| PNEUMA | "And Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at the command of YHWH, and he died there in the fortieth year after the sons of Israel had come out of the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first day of the month." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Aaron the priest went up to Mount Hor at the command of YHWH and died there, in the fortieth year after the sons of Israel had gone out from the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first day of the month." -- (1) "went up to" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "went up" without the preposition (PNEUMA); the Hebrew el indicates direction toward. (2) "at the command of YHWH" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's "by the mouth of YHWH" for al-pi YHWH, consistent with the resolution in v.2. (3) EMET's "of the going out" is a hyper-literal rendering of the infinitive construct; "after...had gone out" (LOGOS) is clearer English. PNEUMA's "had come out" substitutes a different verb. (4) EMET's "[day]" in brackets is supplied; LOGOS/PNEUMA write "first day" explicitly, which is adopted for readability.

---

### 33:39 -- "at his dying on Mount Hor" vs. "when he died on Mount Hor"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Aaron [was] one hundred and twenty-three years old at his dying on Mount Hor." |
| LOGOS | "And Aaron was one hundred and twenty-three years old when he died on Mount Hor." |
| PNEUMA | "And Aaron was one hundred and twenty-three years old when he died on Mount Hor." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Aaron was one hundred and twenty-three years old when he died on Mount Hor." -- EMET's "at his dying" is a hyper-literal rendering of the Hebrew infinitive construct bemotho; "when he died" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is natural English that preserves the temporal sense. Adopted by majority.

---

### 33:40 -- "the Canaanite, the king of Arad" vs. "the Canaanite king of Arad"; "and he [was] dwelling" vs. "and he was dwelling" vs. "who dwelt"; "Negeb" vs. "Negev"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the Canaanite, the king of Arad -- and he [was] dwelling in the Negeb in the land of Canaan -- heard of the coming of the sons of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And the Canaanite, the king of Arad -- and he was dwelling in the Negev in the land of Canaan -- heard of the coming of the sons of Israel." |
| PNEUMA | "And the Canaanite king of Arad -- who dwelt in the Negev, in the land of Canaan -- heard of the coming of the sons of Israel." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the Canaanite, the king of Arad -- and he was dwelling in the Negev in the land of Canaan -- heard of the coming of the sons of Israel." -- (1) "the Canaanite, the king of Arad" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew apposition with two distinct noun phrases (hakken'ani melekh Arad); PNEUMA collapses them into "the Canaanite king of Arad." (2) EMET/LOGOS preserve the Hebrew parenthetical with "and he was dwelling" (vehu yoshev); PNEUMA's "who dwelt" converts this to a relative clause, which smooths the syntax but loses the Hebrew disjunctive construction. (3) "Negev" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the standard modern transliteration; EMET's "Negeb" follows an older convention. "Negev" is adopted.

---

### 33:44 -- "on the border of Moab" vs. "on the border of Moab" (agreement, no divergence)

All three translators agree on this verse. No disagreement recorded.

---

### 33:48 -- "in the plains of Moab" vs. "on the plains of Moab"; "by the Jordan [at] Jericho" vs. "by the Jordan at Jericho" vs. "by the Jordan across from Jericho"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in the plains of Moab, by the Jordan [at] Jericho" |
| LOGOS | "in the plains of Moab, by the Jordan at Jericho" |
| PNEUMA | "on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in the plains of Moab, by the Jordan at Jericho" -- (1) "in" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "on" (PNEUMA) for be'arvot; the Hebrew preposition be denotes "in." (2) "at Jericho" (EMET/LOGOS) is the standard rendering of Yereicho in this construction; PNEUMA's "across from Jericho" interprets the geography (the plains of Moab are across the Jordan from Jericho), but the Hebrew simply places the Jordan "at" Jericho without specifying relative position. This resolution applies also to vv. 49-50.

---

### 33:49 -- "to Abel-shittim" vs. "as far as Abel-shittim"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim, in the plains of Moab" |
| LOGOS | "from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim, in the plains of Moab" |
| PNEUMA | "from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim, on the plains of Moab" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim, in the plains of Moab" -- "As far as" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better renders the Hebrew ad, which expresses extent, than EMET's simple "to." PNEUMA's "on the plains" is resolved per v.48 ("in the plains").

---

### 33:50 -- "in the plains" vs. "on the plains"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in the plains of Moab by the Jordan [at] Jericho" |
| LOGOS | "in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho" |
| PNEUMA | "on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho" -- Resolved per vv.48-49. EMET and LOGOS agree; PNEUMA's "on" and "across from" are divergent.

---

### 33:52 -- "carved stones" vs. "carved images" vs. "cast images" vs. "cast metal figures" vs. "molten images"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall destroy all their carved stones, and all their molten images you shall destroy, and all their high places you shall demolish" |
| LOGOS | "you shall destroy all their carved stones, and all their cast images you shall destroy, and all their high places you shall demolish" |
| PNEUMA | "you shall destroy all their carved images, and all their cast metal figures you shall destroy, and all their high places you shall demolish" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you shall destroy all their carved stones, and all their cast images you shall destroy, and all their high places you shall demolish" -- (1) For maskiyyotam: "carved stones" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "carved images." The Hebrew maskith refers specifically to stone reliefs or figured stones, not images in general. (2) For tsalmei massekhotam: "cast images" (LOGOS) is adopted as a middle ground between EMET's "molten images" (more archaic) and PNEUMA's "cast metal figures" (overly specific). The Hebrew tselem massekah denotes an image made by casting/pouring metal; "cast images" captures both the manufacturing process and the object.

---

### 33:53 -- "dispossess [the inhabitants of]" vs. "dispossess the inhabitants of" vs. "take possession of"; "dwell" vs. "settle"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall dispossess [the inhabitants of] the land and dwell in it, for to you I have given the land to possess it." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land and dwell in it, for to you I have given the land to possess it." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall take possession of the land and settle in it, for I have given the land to you to possess it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land and dwell in it, for to you I have given the land to possess it." -- (1) The Hebrew vehorashtem et ha-arets is ambiguous: the hiphil of yarash can mean "dispossess" (the inhabitants) or "take possession of" (the land). EMET and LOGOS read it as "dispossess" (causative); PNEUMA reads "take possession of" (resultative). The hiphil form in context with the preceding verse's command to "drive out" the inhabitants favors the causative sense: "dispossess." (2) "dwell" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "settle" (PNEUMA) for viyshavtem; "dwell" is more literal. (3) EMET/LOGOS preserve the Hebrew word order "to you I have given" (lakhem natatti); PNEUMA reverses to "I have given...to you." The fronted dative is retained.

---

### 33:54 -- "divide" vs. "apportion"; "for an inheritance among your families" vs. "according to your clans" vs. "as an inheritance among your clans"; "to the many you shall make great his inheritance" vs. "to the larger you shall give a larger inheritance" vs. "to the larger group you shall give a larger inheritance"; "wherever the lot falls to him" vs. "wherever the lot falls for each one" vs. "wherever the lot falls for each person"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall divide the land by lot for an inheritance among your families: to the many you shall make great his inheritance, and to the few you shall make small his inheritance; wherever the lot falls to him, there it shall be his. According to the tribes of your fathers you shall inherit." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall apportion the land by lot according to your clans. To the larger you shall give a larger inheritance, and to the smaller you shall give a smaller inheritance. Wherever the lot falls for each one, there it shall be his. According to the tribes of your fathers you shall receive an inheritance." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall divide the land by lot as an inheritance among your clans. To the larger group you shall give a larger inheritance, and to the smaller group you shall give a smaller inheritance. Wherever the lot falls for each person, that shall be his. You shall receive your inheritance according to the tribes of your fathers." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall apportion the land by lot for an inheritance among your clans. To the larger you shall give a larger inheritance, and to the smaller you shall give a smaller inheritance. Wherever the lot falls for each one, there it shall be his. According to the tribes of your fathers you shall receive an inheritance." -- (1) "apportion" (LOGOS) is adopted over "divide" (EMET/PNEUMA) for vehitnachaltem; the hithpael of nachal means to receive/distribute as inheritance, not merely divide. (2) "for an inheritance" (EMET) is combined with "among your clans" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than EMET's "families" for mishpechotekhem; "clans" is more precise for the Hebrew mishpachah, which denotes a sub-tribal kinship unit. (3) "To the larger...to the smaller" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's hyper-literal "to the many...to the few" and PNEUMA's expanded "to the larger group...to the smaller group." (4) EMET's "make great his inheritance / make small his inheritance" preserves the Hebrew hiphil causatives (tarbeh/tam'it) but is awkward English; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "give a larger/smaller inheritance" is clearer. (5) "for each one" (LOGOS) is adopted as a middle ground. (6) PNEUMA restructures the final clause, moving "according to the tribes of your fathers" to the end and making it modify "receive your inheritance"; the LOGOS word order better preserves the Hebrew. (7) "receive an inheritance" (LOGOS) is adopted over "inherit" (EMET); the hithpael form implies receiving rather than simple inheriting.

---

### 33:55 -- "And if" vs. "But if"; "those whom you leave of them" vs. "those whom you allow to remain of them" vs. "those you allow to remain"; "pricks in your eyes" vs. "barbs in your eyes"; "thorns in your sides" (agreement); "shall trouble you upon the land in which you [are] dwelling" vs. "shall trouble you in the land in which you are dwelling" vs. "will harass you in the land where you are dwelling"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then it shall be [that] those whom you leave of them [shall be] as pricks in your eyes and as thorns in your sides, and they shall trouble you upon the land in which you [are] dwelling." |
| LOGOS | "But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those whom you allow to remain of them shall be as barbs in your eyes and as thorns in your sides, and they shall trouble you in the land in which you are dwelling." |
| PNEUMA | "But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those you allow to remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides, and they will harass you in the land where you are dwelling." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those whom you allow to remain of them shall be as barbs in your eyes and as thorns in your sides, and they shall trouble you in the land in which you are dwelling." -- (1) "But if" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "And if" (EMET) for ve'im; the adversative sense is appropriate given the shift from command to warning. (2) "those whom you allow to remain of them" (LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew participial construction (totiru mehem) with both the relative pronoun and "of them"; EMET's "those whom you leave of them" is close but "allow to remain" better captures the hiphil causative; PNEUMA drops "of them." (3) "barbs" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "pricks" (EMET) for sikkim; both are defensible, but "barbs" is more vivid and widely understood. (4) PNEUMA drops the comparative "as" before both metaphors and uses "will become" instead of "shall be as"; the Hebrew le ("as/for") with the predicative construction supports "shall be as" (EMET/LOGOS). (5) "trouble" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "harass" (PNEUMA) for tsareru; the Hebrew root tsarar means to press, vex, or trouble. (6) "upon the land" (EMET) follows the Hebrew al, but "in the land" (LOGOS) is more natural English for habitation context; adopted. (7) "in which" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "where" (PNEUMA) for maintaining formal register.

---

### 33:56 -- "as I purposed" vs. "as I intended" vs. "what I had planned"; "I shall do" vs. "I will do"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And it shall be [that] as I purposed to do to them, I shall do to you." |
| LOGOS | "And it shall be that as I intended to do to them, I shall do to you." |
| PNEUMA | "And what I had planned to do to them, I will do to you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it shall be that as I purposed to do to them, I shall do to you." -- (1) EMET and LOGOS preserve the introductory formula "And it shall be that" (vehayah ka'asher); PNEUMA omits this entirely, starting with "what I had planned." The formula is part of the Hebrew text and must be retained. (2) "purposed" (EMET) is adopted over "intended" (LOGOS) and "planned" (PNEUMA) for dimmiti; the Hebrew root damah in the pi'el means to think, purpose, or devise. "Purposed" carries the stronger sense of divine determination. (3) "I shall do" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "I will do" (PNEUMA); "shall" preserves the formal/prophetic register appropriate to divine speech.
